region_of_origin: Northeast China (Manchuria); global commercial revival from USA/Europe; widely sold in Singapore
food_subgroup: Fermented tea beverage — SCOBY-fermented sweet tea; low calorie, low sugar, probiotic cultures

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI specific to kombucha. Closest parent: fermented tea beverage. FoodOn gap noted.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to commercial bottled kombucha; A044Z used as closest defensible parent for fermented tea category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for kombucha beverage.

main_ingredients: SCOBY (symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard commercial bottle of 250ml, as sold at Cold Storage, FairPrice Finest, and health food stores in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Kombucha — USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central Branded Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (GT's Living Foods and similar commercial kombucha entries) representing the typical 250ml commercial bottled kombucha profile. Brand variability is noted; moderate confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the USDA branded food entries for kombucha — these fields are set to -1 (not reported).

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Kombucha — analogous estimate based on low residual sugar and organic acid content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous basis: low-sugar fermented beverages)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for commercial kombucha found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~25 is an analogous estimate: residual sugar is ~6g per 250ml (below GI-significant threshold); organic acids (acetic, gluconic) slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose response. Consistent with USDA Branded Food sugar data showing 2.4g sugar per 100ml (6g per 250ml bottle).

hypertension.guidance: At 12mg sodium per 250ml serving, kombucha poses no meaningful sodium burden for hypertensive patients and can be consumed freely as a beverage choice.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low residual sugar (2.4g/100ml), GI ~25, GL ~1.5 per 250ml bottle
type2_diabetes.guidance: Unsweetened or low-sugar commercial kombucha has a very low glycaemic load (~1.5 per bottle) and is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes patients; patients should verify label sugar content as values vary by brand.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, trace antioxidants from tea base
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kombucha contains no fat or saturated fat and the tea-derived polyphenols may provide modest antioxidant benefit, making it a cardiovascularly neutral to slightly positive beverage choice.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kombucha is a commercially bottled fermented tea beverage made by fermenting sweetened black or green tea with a SCOBY (symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast). The fermentation converts most of the added sugar into organic acids (acetic, gluconic), carbon dioxide, and trace ethanol, resulting in a lightly effervescent, tart, and tangy drink with 14 kcal per 100ml. It has negligible fat and protein. Key clinical facts: very low residual sugar (~2.4g/100ml), GI ~25, GL ~1.5 per 250ml bottle, trace ethanol (0.3–0.5% ABV — below Singapore's 1.2% non-alcoholic threshold). Halal status uncertain; JAKIM advises caution for observant Muslims due to trace alcohol. Contraindicated for pregnant women and immunocompromised individuals due to live, unpasteurised cultures. Probiotic health claims are Grade D evidence — the cultures are acetic acid bacteria and yeast, not established probiotic strains such as Lactobacillus. Popular in Singapore health food stores including FairPrice Finest, Cold Storage, and specialty retailers. Source: USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods.

SCOBY: symbiotic culture of bacteria and yeast

confidence_reason: Nutritional values adapted from USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods; high variability between brands in sugar, acid, and trace nutrient content. GI is analogous estimate, not directly measured. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not reported in the source — these fields are set to -1 (not reported by USDA Branded Food entries for kombucha). Moderate confidence overall.
notes: Halal status uncertain — JAKIM recommends caution due to trace ethanol. Unpasteurised: contraindicated for pregnant women and immunocompromised individuals. Probiotic health claims unsubstantiated at clinical level. Brand variability is significant — always verify label. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water content are not listed in source (USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods for kombucha) — set to -1 (not reported). Fat_g=0.0 confirmed zero — pure fermented tea beverage with no added fat. Dietary_fibre_g=0.0 confirmed zero — no fibre in fermented tea. Vitamin_a, folate, vitamin_d, vitamin_e, zinc, selenium, cholesterol all confirmed as zero or trace as reported by USDA source. G4 core macro sanity: fat_g=0.0 is defensible for a pure fermented tea beverage with no added fat; carbohydrate_g=3.2 and energy_kcal=14.0 are consistent with low-sugar fermented tea.
